title: Lists: indexing, slicing, methods

overview.what:
A list is an ordered, mutable sequence of any Python objects. You create one with square brackets and access elements by zero-based index — negative indexes count from the end:

  items = ['a', 'b', 'c', 'd']
  items[0]   # 'a' — first
  items[-1]  # 'd' — last

Slicing returns a new list — it does NOT modify the original:

  items[1:3]  # ['b', 'c'] — index 1 up to (not including) 3
  items[::-1] # ['d', 'c', 'b', 'a'] — reversed copy

Key methods (all mutate in place, return None unless noted):
  append(x) — add x to the end
  extend([x, y]) — add all items from an iterable
  pop(i) — remove and RETURN item at index i (default: last)
  insert(i, x) — insert x before index i
  remove(x) — remove the first occurrence of x (ValueError if absent)
  sort() — sort in place; sorted(lst) returns a new sorted list
  index(x) — return the first index of x (ValueError if absent)

Think of a list as a numbered row of slots — you can insert, delete, and reorder freely because slots shift to close the gap.
overview.why: Lists are the default container for ordered data in Python: API response arrays, database result sets, pipeline stages, log entries. Knowing slicing and the right method prevents off-by-one bugs and unexpected None returns.

why_learning_this: Slicing API result lists (page = results[offset:offset+limit]) for pagination in FastAPI endpoints
Using pop() and append() correctly — both return None by accident is one of the most common beginner bugs

common_mistakes.title: Assigning the return value of append or sort
common_mistakes.explanation: result = items.append(x) sets result to None because append mutates in place and returns None. These mutating methods return nothing useful — call them as statements.

common_mistakes.title: Slicing thinking it modifies the original
common_mistakes.explanation: items[1:3] returns a NEW list — the original is unchanged. To replace a slice in place, assign to the slice: items[1:3] = ['x', 'y'].

common_mistakes.title: Using remove() without checking membership first
common_mistakes.explanation: items.remove(x) raises ValueError if x is not in the list. Guard with if x in items: or catch ValueError.

recall_questions.q: What does a negative index like -1 mean on a list?
recall_questions.answer: It counts from the end — -1 is the last element, -2 is second-to-last.

recall_questions.q: Does slicing a list modify the original list?
recall_questions.answer: No — slicing always returns a new list; the original is unchanged.
It removes AND returns the item at index i (the last item when no index is given).
None — it mutates the list in place and returns nothing useful.

practice_tasks.title: Paginate a result list
practice_tasks.prompt: Write paginate(items, page, page_size) that returns the correct slice of items for the given page (0-based). Print results for page 0 and page 1 of a 10-item list with page_size=3.
practice_tasks.solution:
def paginate(items, page, page_size):
    start = page * page_size
    return items[start:start + page_size]

records = [f'record-{i}' for i in range(10)]
print(paginate(records, 0, 3))
print(paginate(records, 1, 3))

code_walkthrough.code:
log = ['INFO', 'DEBUG', 'ERROR', 'INFO', 'WARN']
print(log[0])
print(log[-1])
print(log[1:3])
log.append('ERROR')
log.sort()
print(log)
code_walkthrough.focus: log[1:3] returns a new list without changing log; append and sort mutate log in place — the final print shows both effects accumulated.

challenge.title: Request queue manager
challenge.prompt: Write enqueue(queue, item) and dequeue(queue) functions that treat a list as a FIFO queue. enqueue appends to the end; dequeue removes and returns from the front, returning None when the queue is empty (do not raise). Print three enqueue calls, then dequeue until empty.
challenge.solution:
def enqueue(queue, item):
    queue.append(item)

def dequeue(queue):
    return queue.pop(0) if queue else None

q = []
enqueue(q, 'req-1')
enqueue(q, 'req-2')
enqueue(q, 'req-3')
print(dequeue(q))
print(dequeue(q))
print(dequeue(q))
print(dequeue(q))
